learning_outcomes.description: Understand the role and key operational functions of an international hospitality business.
Become cognisant of the operational management challenges and demands of international hoteliers.
Demonstrate an understanding of the importance of guest experience, and apply research to identify key components and analyse critical features of guests' stay experience in the hotel sector.
Work in teams, discuss and present contemporary operational issues in the hotel industry.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment aims to comprehensively evaluate students’ understanding of global hotel industry operations. It focuses on assessing their ability to engage effectively with industry professionals, as well as their reflective learning and practical coordination skills within a real-world context. This assessment involves the following components:

#1 Student-Led Mini-Industry Forum (Group 50%)

Starting from Week 4, industry experts will be invited to deliver 30–40-minute guest talks each week on specific industry trends, problems, or best practices in hospitality operations.

Each student team will be responsible for designing and delivering a 15-minute presentation on a real-world problem, preparing relevant questions for guest speakers, and actively participating in the Q&A session, including responding to questions asked by the guests. 

Content and Analytical Quality (25%): Students will work in small groups to identify and analyse a real-world problem in the hospitality industry. Drawing on course concepts, each team will present their findings and proposed solutions and recommendations during a 15-minute in-class presentation. 

Presentation design, communication and engagement (25%): Students are expected to actively listen, take notes, and participate in the subsequent Q&A session. Following the industry expert’s presentations, student teams will take the lead in asking questions and engaging in a deeper discussion of the topic. 

Students will be evaluated on their design and delivery of the presentation, the quality and relevance of the questions they ask, as well as their critical analysis and responses to industry expert’s answers. 

To support each team, the teaching staff will offer consultation, ensuring that students receive the guidance and resources necessary to succeed. Presentation order will be scheduled during the semester, aligned with the chosen topics and availability of industry guest speakers. 

Evaluation criteria and marking sheets will be provided in blackboard.

#2 Teamwork evaluation & reflection (to modify team performance)

Teamwork evaluation and reflection will be assessed. All team members must complete a ‘peer evaluation’ for each of their team members and submit online in your Blackboard site through the platform Buddycheck. The purpose of this evaluation and reflection process is to provide an opportunity to learn about how each student contributed to the team project. Collaborative skills are a basic graduate attribute that all students should enter the workplace with. The assessment criteria for collaborative skills are:

1) Contribution – team member contributed to the process required to complete the assignment.

2) Knowledge – team member contributed knowledge, opinions, and skills to team discussions and work completion.

3) Collaboration & Communication – team member collaborated and communicated with others in the team and fulfilled their individual roles within the team.

4) Responsibility – team member accepted responsibility for the team’s assignment outcome.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Online submission of PPT slides, and a list of questions prepared for industry guests (via Blackboard)
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Individual Learning Reflection: Hospitality Operations - Part 1 - Written Essays
assessments.due_date:
Essay 1 - Week 7, Fri 2:00 pm

Essay 2 - Week 11, Fri 2:00 pm
assessments.task_description:
This assessment encourages critical reflection and application of theory to practice in hospitality operations. Students will complete two reflective essays (15% each) and a one-on-one interactive online oral interview (20%). The essays integrate personal experiences, theoretical frameworks, and insights from weekly industry forums and team projects.

Part 1: Two Reflective Written Essays (30%)

Students will submit two reflective essays, each addressing a different hospitality operations challenge, at the following intervals: Weeks 4-7, Weeks 8-11. Each essay should be no more than two pages in length.

Essay Structure: Each essay (around 600 words, 2 pages) should include the following sections:

1. Problem Definition and Personal Context (approx. 150 words): Clearly articulate a specific hospitality operations challenge. Ground your analysis in relevant course lectures and industry forum discussions. Integrate a personal experience (as a guest, employee, or through a hypothetical scenario) to illustrate the problem and provide context for your reflection. Explain the significance of this challenge within the broader context of effective hotel management.

2. Theoretical Framework and Analysis (approx. 150 words): Introduce a relevant theoretical framework from any discipline (e.g., management, marketing, psychology, sociology, economics) that you believe illuminates the chosen hotel operations issue. Apply this framework to analyse the chosen challenge, integrating insights from the industry forum. Explain how the theory illuminates the problem, highlighting relevant concepts and justifying your choice of framework. Do not just summarise the theory; show how its core principles can be applied to the hospitality context. Critically analyse any discrepancies between theory and practice. Consider different perspectives or interpretations.

3. Proposed Solutions and Reflective Narrative (approx. 150 words): Based on your analysis, propose practical solutions or improvements to address the identified challenge. Integrate insights from the industry forum and relate your proposed solutions to your personal experience and the theoretical framework. How might your personal experiences inform the implementation of these solutions? Discuss the potential challenges and limitations of your proposed solutions, demonstrating critical thinking.

4. Personal Learning and Future Applications (approx. 150 words): Summarise your key learning outcomes from this reflection. Discuss how your understanding of the challenge has evolved, any new perspectives gained, and unanswered questions. Explain how you will apply this learning to future coursework or your career aspirations.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Written esssays will be submitted through Blackboard. More details will be discussed in class.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Individual Learning Reflection: Hospitality Operations - Part 2 - Interactive online oral reflection interview
assessments.due_date:
Week 13

The interactive online oral reflection interview will take place during Week 13, with students booking an appointment time in advance.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment encourages critical reflection and application of theory to practice in hospitality operations. Students will complete two reflective essays (15% each) and a one-on-one interactive online oral interview (20%). The essays integrate personal experiences, theoretical frameworks, and insights from weekly industry forums and team projects.

Part 2: Interactive online oral reflection interview (20%) (Week 13)

During the 10-minute individual online interview, each student will first deliver a two-minute oral presentation summarising their key learning from the industry forums. Discuss how your understanding of hospitality operations has evolved and how you plan to apply this knowledge in your future career. This oral reflection should be concise, engaging, and reflective of your personal learning experience throughout the course. Further discussion regarding the presentation order will be provided in class.

After your two-minute oral presentation, you will be asked 2-3 questions designed to gauge your understanding of hospitality operations and critical thinking skills related to industry challenges discussed during this course.

Interview Guidelines:

  • Be Punctual: Join the online (Zoom or Microsoft Teams) meeting at your scheduled time.
  • Be Prepared: Have your notes and course materials readily available.
  • Be Concise: Answer the questions thoughtfully and concisely, providing specific examples where appropriate.
  • Be Reflective: Demonstrate your understanding by explaining your reasoning and reflecting on your learning process.
  • Be Honest: If you are unsure about a concept, acknowledge it honestly and explain what you understand.

A detailed rubric outlining the assessment criteria will be provided separately.

Please Note: The presentation will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
The Future of Hospitality: Navigating Disruption and Emerging Trends

This week, we will cover:

  • Macro-level trends impacting the industry (e.g., climate change, geopolitical instability, technological advancements).
  • Guest expectations and evolving travel patterns.
  • The rise of alternative accommodations and its impact on traditional hotels.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: The Future of Hospitality: Navigating Disruption and Emerging Trends

Guest expectations and evolving travel patterns. The rise of alternative accommodations and its impact on traditional hotels.

learning_activities.topic:
Hotel Operations Model: Operational Resilience in a Volatile Market

This week, we focus on hotel operations models:

  • Examine how different strategies contribute to overall performance across three key perspectives: workforce, customer, and organisation.
  • Analyse seven key management areas crucial for success.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Wellness, Longevity & New Demand Segments

Demand is fragmenting into lifestyle-driven segments, such as:

  • Wellness tourism operations (spa, sleep tech, nutrition)
  • Ageing travellers & “longevity economy”
  • Remote work / digital nomads (beyond serviced apartments)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
